OrbitumAI
00Start Here✓ 01Install Tools✓ 02GitHub Setup✓ 03Supabase Setup✓ 04Vercel Setup✓ 05Project Structure✓ 06build.md✓ 07prd.md✓ 08Agent Files✓ 09Configure Cursor✓ 10Connect All✓ 11What's Next✓
0 of 12 complete
Ex-Apple PM • Built for Non-Technical Founders

From Idea to Deployed App — Zero Code Experience Required

This guide walks you through every tool, every file, and every step to go from a blank folder to a live AI-powered product. No DevOps. No guesswork. No gatekeeping.

Director Ai
Founder, OrbitumAI — 25yr enterprise + AI builder
Estimated completion: ~4 hours
CursorGitHubNode.jsTypeScriptSupabaseVercel
Start Here →
Mark this section complete
Section 01

Install Your Builder Stack

PM Note

Think of this as setting up your workshop before building furniture. Every tool has one job. Don't skip any. Install in this exact order.

✎ 1. Cursor Download →
Your AI-powered code editor. This is where you'll spend 90% of your time building.
  1. Go to cursor.com/download
  2. Download for your OS (Mac: .dmg / Windows: .exe)
  3. Open installer, drag to Applications (Mac) or run installer (Windows)
  4. Launch Cursor
  5. Sign in with GitHub (create a GitHub account first if needed)
  6. Skip the tour for now — you'll configure it in Section 9
✓ Verify

Cursor opens and you see the AI chat panel on the right side.

⚠ Common Pitfall

Don't use VS Code for this guide. Cursor is VS Code with AI built-in — same shortcuts, but with superpowers.

⚙ 2. Node.js (LTS) Download →
The JavaScript engine that runs your app locally and powers all your build tools.
  1. Go to nodejs.org
  2. Click the LTS version button (not Current)
  3. Run the installer, accept all defaults
  4. Open Cursor, press Ctrl+` (backtick) to open the terminal
  5. Type the verify commands below
bash
# Should show v20.x.x or higher
node -v

# Should show 10.x.x or higher
npm -v
⚠ Common Pitfall

Always install LTS, never Current. LTS = stable. Current = experimental.

📜 3. TypeScript
A stricter version of JavaScript that catches errors before your users do.
  1. Open Cursor terminal (Ctrl+`)
  2. Run the install command below
  3. Wait for install to complete
bash
npm install -g typescript

# Verify — should show Version 5.x.x
tsc -v
⚠ Common Pitfall

If you get "permission denied" on Mac, prefix with sudo: sudo npm install -g typescript

🔌 4. Git Download →
Tracks every change you make to your code. Your permanent undo button.
  1. Go to git-scm.com/downloads
  2. Download for your OS, run installer with all defaults
  3. Open Cursor terminal and configure your identity:
bash
git config --global user.name "Your Full Name"
git config --global user.email "you@youremail.com"

# Verify — should show git version 2.x.x
git --version
⚠ Common Pitfall

Use the same email you'll use for GitHub. Mismatched emails break commit attribution.

🖥 5. GitHub Desktop Download →
A visual interface for Git. See your changes, commit, and push without memorizing commands. Recommended for beginners.
  1. Go to desktop.github.com
  2. Download and install
  3. Sign in with your GitHub account
  4. This is optional but highly recommended for your first few weeks
✓ Verify

GitHub Desktop opens and shows your repositories.

⚡ 6. Supabase CLI
Lets you control your Supabase database and Edge Functions from the terminal.
  1. Open Cursor terminal
  2. Run the install command below
  3. Wait for install
bash
npm install -g supabase

# Verify
supabase --version
⚠ Common Pitfall

If install fails, try: npm install -g supabase@latest

▲ 7. Vercel CLI
Deploy your app to the internet with a single command.
  1. Open Cursor terminal
  2. Run the install command below
bash
npm install -g vercel

# Verify
vercel --version
🦖 8. Deno Download →
The runtime that powers Supabase Edge Functions. Different from Node.js — lighter and faster.

Mac / Linux:

bash
curl -fsSL https://deno.land/install.sh | sh

Windows (PowerShell):

powershell
irm https://deno.land/install.ps1 | iex

After install, restart Cursor terminal.

bash
# Verify
deno --version
⚠ Common Pitfall

Edge Functions run on Deno, not Node.js. They use URL imports instead of npm packages. This will make sense when you get to Section 10.

Mark this section complete
Section 02

Set Up GitHub — Your Code's Permanent Home

PM Note

GitHub is where your product lives forever. Every change, every version, every rollback lives here. Think of it as Google Drive for code — but with a full history of every edit ever made.

1

1Create your GitHub account

  • Go to github.com → Sign up
  • Use a professional email (this is public-facing for your product)
  • Verify your email
2

2Create a GitHub Organization (recommended)

  • Click your profile photo → Your organizations → New organization
  • Choose Free plan
  • Name it after your product or company (e.g. orbitumai)
  • Why: keeps personal repos separate from product repos. Looks professional.
3

3Create your Repository

  • Go to github.com/new (or org → New repository)
  • Repository name: your product name in lowercase-with-dashes (e.g. my-ai-app)
  • Set to: Private
  • Check: Add a README file
  • Add .gitignore: select Node
  • Choose license: MIT
  • Click Create repository
4

4Clone to your local machine

Method A (GitHub Desktop — easier):

  1. Click Code → Open with GitHub Desktop
  2. Choose where to save it on your computer
  3. Click Clone

Method B (Terminal — faster):

bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPO-NAME.git
cd YOUR-REPO-NAME
5

5Open in Cursor

In Cursor: File → Open Folder → select your cloned repo folder. You should see README.md in the file explorer.

6

6Create your two branches

bash
# You're already on main — this is your production branch
git branch dev
git checkout dev
# Now you're on dev — this is your sandbox
git push -u origin dev

Branching strategy in plain English:

  • main = your live app. Real users see this. Only merge here when something is tested and ready.
  • dev = your sandbox. Break things here. Test here. This is your daily working branch.
diagram
main ──────────────────────────────── [LIVE: real users]
          ↑ merge when ready
dev  ──●──●──●──●──●──●──●──●──●──── [YOUR DAILY WORK]
       f1 f2 f3 fix f4 f5 fix f6 f7
7

7The daily git workflow

bash
# Make sure you're on dev branch
git checkout dev

# After making changes, save them:
git add .
git commit -m "feat: describe what you built"
git push origin dev

# When ready to go live:
git checkout main
git merge dev
git push origin main
8

8Commit message conventions

PrefixUse when
feat:Adding a new feature
fix:Fixing a bug
chore:Config, deps, non-code changes
docs:Updating documentation
refactor:Restructuring code, not changing behavior
Mark this section complete
Section 03

Set Up Supabase — Your Database + Backend

PM Note

Supabase is your app's memory and your backend in one. Users, data, files, authentication — all here. We create 2 projects: one for building and testing, one for real users. Never mix them. This mistake costs founders days of debugging.

1

1Create your account

Go to supabase.com → Start your project → Sign up with GitHub (same account).

2

2Create Organization

Click New organization. Name it after your product or company. Plan: Free (enough to launch).

3

3Create Project #1 — Development

  • Name: [product-name]-dev (e.g. myapp-dev)
  • Database password: click Generate, copy it, save in your password manager
  • Region: choose closest to you
  • Click Create new project (takes ~2 minutes)
4

4Create Project #2 — Production

  • Name: [product-name]-prod
  • Use a different strong password
  • Same region
5

5Get your API keys — for BOTH projects

In Supabase dashboard: Settings → API. Copy these 3 values:

  • Project URL
  • anon/public key (safe for browser)
  • service_role key (treat like a master password — server only, never in browser)
6

6Create your .env.local file

bash
# ── SUPABASE DEV ──────────────────────────────
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://xxxx.supabase.co
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=eyJhb...
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=eyJhb...

# ── SUPABASE PROD (used in Vercel env vars) ───
# NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://yyyy.supabase.co
# NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=eyJhb...
# SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=eyJhb...

# ── AI ────────────────────────────────────────
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

# ── APP ───────────────────────────────────────
NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL=http://localhost:3000
7

7Add .env.local to .gitignore immediately

gitignore
.env.local
.env
supabase/.env
8

8Enable Row Level Security (RLS)

RLS means users can only see their own data. Without it, any logged-in user could read everyone's data. It's like having individual locked drawers instead of one shared filing cabinet. Always enable it.

In Supabase Table Editor: when creating any table → toggle "Enable Row Level Security" ON.

9

9Create your first table (profiles)

In Supabase dashboard → Table Editor → New table:

  • Name: profiles
  • Enable RLS: ON
  • id — UUID, primary key, default: gen_random_uuid()
  • user_id — UUID, foreign key → auth.users.id
  • full_name — text
  • email — text
  • created_at — timestamptz, default: now()
  • updated_at — timestamptz, default: now()
Mark this section complete
Section 04

Set Up Vercel — Your App's Launch Pad

PM Note

Vercel deploys your app to the internet automatically every time you push code. In the old world, this required a DevOps engineer and half a day. Today: 3 minutes. Every push to dev = a preview URL. Every push to main = your live app updated.

1

1Create your account

Go to vercel.com → Sign up with GitHub — use the same GitHub account. This is critical.

2

2Import your repository

Click Add New → Project → Import your repository. If you don't see it, click "Adjust GitHub App Permissions" and grant access.

3

3Configure your project

  • Framework Preset: Next.js (auto-detected)
  • Root Directory: ./
  • Build Command: npm run build
  • Output Directory: .next
  • Install Command: npm install
4

4Add Environment Variables

Before clicking Deploy, add each variable from your .env.local. For each variable, set which environments it applies to:

  • NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL → Production uses prod URL, Preview uses dev URL
  • NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY → same split
  • SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY → same split
  • ANTHROPIC_API_KEY → all environments (same key is fine)
5

5Click Deploy

Watch the build log — understand each phase:

  • Installing dependencies → npm install running
  • Building → TypeScript compiling, Next.js optimizing
  • Deploying → uploading to Vercel's global CDN
6

6How auto-deploy works

diagram
Push to dev   →  Vercel builds  →  Preview URL
Push to main  →  Vercel builds  →  Production URL

Every push creates a unique preview URL. Share it with anyone to test before going live.

7

7Set up custom domain (optional)

  • Vercel Project → Settings → Domains
  • Add your domain (e.g. myapp.com)
  • Copy the DNS records Vercel gives you
  • Add them in your domain registrar (GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare)
  • SSL certificate is automatic — Vercel handles it
Mark this section complete
Section 05

Structure Your Project Like a Pro

PM Note

Folder structure is the skeleton of your product. Get it right once and everything — debugging, AI assistance, scaling — becomes easier forever. This is the exact structure used for production Next.js AI apps.

folder structure
 1my-ai-app/
 2├── .cursor/                          ← Cursor AI agent rules
 3│   ├── rules/
 4│   │   ├── build.md                 ← Always: main build rules
 5│   │   ├── prd.md                   ← Always: product requirements
 6│   │   ├── guardrails.md            ← Always: what never to do
 7│   │   ├── security.md              ← Always: security rules
 8│   │   ├── diagnostic.md            ← Agent Requested: debugging
 9│   │   ├── api-patterns.md          ← Auto: app/api/**
10│   │   ├── db-schema.md             ← Auto: lib/supabase/**
11│   │   ├── edge-functions.md        ← Auto: supabase/functions/**
12│   │   └── ux-patterns.md           ← Auto: components/**
13│   └── mcp.json                      ← MCP server connections
14│
15├── app/                              ← Next.js App Router
16│   ├── (auth)/
17│   │   ├── login/page.tsx
18│   │   └── signup/page.tsx
19│   ├── (dashboard)/
20│   │   ├── layout.tsx
21│   │   └── dashboard/page.tsx
22│   ├── api/
23│   │   ├── ai/route.ts
24│   │   └── webhooks/route.ts
25│   ├── globals.css
26│   └── layout.tsx
27│
28├── components/
29│   ├── ui/                           ← Pure presentational
30│   └── shared/                       ← Business components
31│
32├── lib/
33│   ├── supabase/
34│   │   ├── client.ts                 ← Browser Supabase client
35│   │   ├── server.ts                 ← Server Supabase client
36│   │   └── middleware.ts             ← Auth middleware
37│   ├── ai/claude.ts                  ← Claude API wrapper
38│   └── utils/helpers.ts
39│
40├── supabase/
41│   ├── functions/
42│   │   ├── ai-chat/index.ts          ← Edge Function: AI calls
43│   │   ├── stripe-webhook/index.ts   ← Edge Function: payments
44│   │   └── daily-digest/index.ts     ← Edge Function: cron
45│   ├── migrations/                   ← Database schema history
46│   └── config.toml                   ← Supabase project config
47│
48├── types/index.ts                    ← TypeScript interfaces
49├── public/                           ← Static assets
50├── .env.local                        ← Local secrets (NEVER commit)
51├── .env.example                      ← Template (safe to commit)
52├── .gitignore
53├── middleware.ts                     ← Next.js auth middleware
54├── package.json
55├── tailwind.config.ts
56├── tsconfig.json
57└── README.md

Scaffold commands

bash
# Create all directories
mkdir -p .cursor/rules
mkdir -p app/\(auth\)/login app/\(auth\)/signup
mkdir -p app/\(dashboard\)/dashboard
mkdir -p app/api/ai app/api/webhooks
mkdir -p components/ui components/shared
mkdir -p lib/supabase lib/ai lib/utils
mkdir -p supabase/functions/ai-chat
mkdir -p supabase/functions/stripe-webhook
mkdir -p supabase/functions/daily-digest
mkdir -p supabase/migrations
mkdir -p types public

# Create key files
touch .env.local .env.example
touch types/index.ts
touch lib/supabase/client.ts lib/supabase/server.ts
touch lib/ai/claude.ts
Mark this section complete
Section 06

Create build.md — The Brain of Your Project

PM Note

This is the most important file you'll create. It tells Cursor's AI your entire tech stack, your coding rules, and your architecture. Without this, Cursor gives generic answers. With this, Cursor writes code that fits your product perfectly. Write it once. Update it as your product grows.

Create the file at .cursor/rules/build.md with this content:

markdown
 1---
 2description: Core build rules, tech stack, and architecture for this project.
 3alwaysApply: true
 4---
 5
 6# Build Rules — [Your Product Name]
 7
 8## What This Product Does
 9[2-3 sentences: what it does, who it's for, what problem it solves]
10
11## Tech Stack
12- Framework: Next.js 14+ (App Router)
13- Language: TypeScript (strict mode)
14- Database: Supabase (PostgreSQL + Auth + Storage + Edge Functions)
15- Deployment: Vercel (dev → preview, main → production)
16- Styling: Tailwind CSS
17- State: Zustand (client state only)
18- AI: Anthropic Claude API (claude-sonnet-4-5)
19- Edge Functions: Supabase Edge Functions (Deno runtime)
20- Forms: React Hook Form + Zod validation
21
22## Architecture Rules
23- Default to Server Components. 'use client' only for browser APIs/state.
24- All DB calls through /lib/supabase/ — never in components.
25- All AI calls through Edge Functions or /lib/ai/ — never expose keys.
26- Route handlers in /app/api/ — keep thin (validate → lib → respond).
27- Business logic in /lib/ — not in components or routes.
28- RLS on every Supabase table — no exceptions.
29
30## Code Style
31- No `any` types — ask for the correct type.
32- Functional components only — no class components.
33- Named exports except Next.js pages (default export).
34- kebab-case files, PascalCase components.
35- Files under 200 lines — extract if larger.
36
37## Environment Variables
38- NEXT_PUBLIC_ = browser safe (no secrets)
39- No prefix = server only (secrets here)
40- Edge Functions: Deno.env.get('KEY')
41- Never hardcode URLs, keys, or secrets.
42
43## Error Handling
44- Every async: try/catch.
45- User-facing: friendly message.
46- Server: console.error('[fn]:', error).
47- Always handle: loading, error, empty states.
48
49## When Stuck
501. Check /diagnostic.md
512. Check /guardrails.md
523. API issues → /api-patterns.md
534. DB issues → /db-schema.md
545. Edge Functions → /edge-functions.md
Mark this section complete
Section 07

Create prd.md — Define What You're Building

PM Note

At Apple, no engineer wrote a single line of code without a PRD. This is your product bible. The clearer you write this, the better Cursor understands what to build. Spend 30 minutes here before you write one line of code. It saves days.

Create at .cursor/rules/prd.md. Replace every [bracket] with your actual product info:

markdown
 1---
 2description: Product requirements. Reference when building features.
 3alwaysApply: true
 4---
 5
 6# Product Requirements Document
 7**Product:** [Your Product Name]
 8**Version:** 1.0
 9**Owner:** [Your Name / Company]
10
11## 1. Problem Statement
12[What problem does this solve? Be specific.]
13
14## 2. Target User
15**Persona:** [e.g. "Solo SaaS founder, non-technical"]
16**Key Pain:** [One sentence]
17**Current Solution:** [What they use today]
18**Why It Fails:** [One sentence]
19
20## 3. Product Vision
21The product exists to [do what] so that [who] can [outcome]
22without [painful thing they currently deal with].
23
24## 4. Core Features — MVP Only
25### Feature 1: [Name]
26**User Story:** As a [persona], I want to [action] so that [benefit].
27**Acceptance Criteria:**
28- [ ] Criterion 1
29- [ ] Criterion 2
30
31### Feature 2: [Name]
32[repeat]
33
34## 5. Not In V1
35- [Feature to defer]
36- [Another to defer]
37Reason: Validate core value first.
38
39## 6. Success Metrics
40**Activation:** [First session success]
41**Retention:** [What brings them back]
42**Revenue:** [How it makes money]
43**North Star:** [One key metric]
44
45## 7. Technical Constraints
46- Mobile functional (375px min)
47- Page load < 3s
48- AI response < 8s
49
50## 8. Integrations
51- Supabase: auth + data
52- Anthropic Claude: [AI use case]
53
54## 9. User Flow (Happy Path)
551. User lands on homepage
562. [Next step]
573. [Reaches value moment]
584. [Converts or returns]
Mark this section complete
Section 08

Create Your Agent Files — Cursor's Specialist Team

PM Note

Each file is a specialist. build.md is your architect. diagnostic.md is your debugger. security.md is your CISO. edge-functions.md is your backend engineer. Together they make Cursor behave like a 5-person engineering team that never forgets your rules.

guardrails.md

Hard rules for what to never do. Prevents the most common and expensive mistakes before they happen.

markdown
 1---
 2description: Hard rules — what to never do.
 3alwaysApply: true
 4---
 5
 6# Guardrails
 7
 8## Never Do These
 9- Never commit .env.local to GitHub
10- Never use `any` type in TypeScript
11- Never call service_role key from browser
12- Never call AI API keys from browser — use Edge Functions
13- Never store secrets in code files
14- Never expose raw errors to users
15- Never skip loading/error/empty states
16- Never deploy to main without testing on dev
17- Never delete Supabase tables without backup
18- Never use dangerouslySetInnerHTML without DOMPurify
19- Never trust user input — validate server-side with Zod
20- Never put secrets in Edge Function code — use supabase secrets set
21- Never skip CORS headers in Edge Functions
22
23## Always Do These
24- Add .env.local to .gitignore before first commit
25- Enable RLS before any table goes live
26- Handle error + loading states in every async op
27- Test mobile (375px) before shipping
28- Push to dev first, test, then merge to main
29- Use separate Supabase projects: dev and prod
30- Set Edge Function secrets separately for dev/prod
31
32## Code Quality
33- File > 200 lines → refactor
34- Function does 2+ things → split
35- Copy-paste twice → extract to /lib/utils/
36- API call in component → move to /lib/

diagnostic.md

Debugging rules and common error patterns. Cursor uses this to diagnose errors systematically.

markdown
 1---
 2description: Debugging and error diagnosis rules.
 3alwaysApply: false
 4---
 5
 6# Diagnostic Rules
 7
 8## When ANY Error Occurs
 91. Read the full error message
102. Identify: error type + file + line number
113. TypeScript error, runtime, build, or network?
124. Check browser console AND terminal
135. Check if recent change caused it (git diff)
14
15## Common Errors
16
17**"Cannot read properties of undefined"**
18→ Add optional chaining: data?.property
19
20**"Module not found"**
21→ Check import path, tsconfig.json paths, exact casing
22
23**"relation does not exist"**
24→ Table name mismatch — Supabase is case-sensitive
25
26**"Environment variable is undefined"**
27→ Check .env.local, NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix, restart dev server
28
29**"Hydration error"**
30→ Move dynamic content to useEffect or dynamic import
31
32**Edge Function: no response**
33→ CORS headers missing — add to ALL responses incl. errors
34
35**Edge Function: Deno.env undefined**
36→ Secret not set — run supabase secrets set KEY=value
37
38## Debug Checklist
39- [ ] Full error read
40- [ ] Browser console checked
41- [ ] Terminal logs checked
42- [ ] .env.local verified
43- [ ] Dev server restarted
44- [ ] .next cache cleared
45- [ ] Supabase dashboard checked
46- [ ] Edge Function logs checked

security.md

Security rules for auth, API routes, database, and Edge Functions. Non-negotiable for production apps.

markdown
 1---
 2description: Security rules for auth, APIs, DB, and Edge Functions.
 3alwaysApply: true
 4---
 5
 6# Security Rules
 7
 8## Auth
 9- Use Supabase Auth only — never roll your own
10- Always use getUser() not getSession() for security checks
11- Protect routes via middleware.ts
12
13## API Routes
14- Every route: verify user, return 401 if none
15- Validate inputs with Zod
16- Rate limit AI endpoints (Upstash Ratelimit)
17
18## Database
19- RLS on every table
20- Users access own rows only: auth.uid() = user_id
21- service_role only server-side
22
23## Edge Functions
24- Verify auth at start of every function
25- Webhooks: verify signature, not auth
26- All secrets via Deno.env.get()
27- --no-verify-jwt only for public webhooks
28
29## Env Vars
30- NEXT_PUBLIC_ = browser safe, no secrets
31- No prefix = server only, secrets here
32- Separate keys for dev and prod
33- npm audit weekly

api-patterns.md

Standard patterns for Next.js API routes. Auto-attached when you're working in app/api/.

markdown
 1---
 2description: API route patterns. Auto-attached for app/api/ files.
 3alwaysApply: false
 4globs: ["app/api/**/*.ts"]
 5---
 6
 7# API Route Patterns
 8
 9## Standard Route Template
10```typescript
11import { createClient } from '@/lib/supabase/server'
12import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from 'next/server'
13import { z } from 'zod'
14
15const schema = z.object({ /* inputs */ })
16
17export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
18  try {
19    const supabase = createClient()
20    const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser()
21    if (!user) return NextResponse.json(
22      { error: 'Unauthorized' }, { status: 401 })
23
24    const body = await request.json()
25    const parsed = schema.safeParse(body)
26    if (!parsed.success) return NextResponse.json(
27      { error: 'Invalid input' }, { status: 400 })
28
29    // Business logic here
30    return NextResponse.json({ data: result })
31  } catch (error) {
32    console.error('[route] POST:', error)
33    return NextResponse.json(
34      { error: 'Internal server error' }, { status: 500 })
35  }
36}
37```
38
39## Calling Edge Functions from API Routes
40```typescript
41const { data, error } = await supabase.functions.invoke(
42  'function-name', { body: { key: value } })
43```

db-schema.md

Database naming conventions, RLS templates, and migration rules.

markdown
 1---
 2description: Database naming, migrations, and RLS templates.
 3alwaysApply: false
 4globs: ["lib/supabase/**/*.ts", "supabase/migrations/**/*.sql"]
 5---
 6
 7# Database Schema Rules
 8
 9## Naming
10- Tables: snake_case, plural (users, projects)
11- Columns: snake_case (user_id, created_at)
12- PKs: always id UUID
13- FKs: [singular]_id (user_id, project_id)
14- Booleans: is_ or has_ prefix
15- Every table: created_at + updated_at
16
17## RLS Template
18```sql
19ALTER TABLE your_table ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
20
21CREATE POLICY "Users can view own data"
22  ON your_table FOR SELECT
23  USING (auth.uid() = user_id);
24
25CREATE POLICY "Users can insert own data"
26  ON your_table FOR INSERT
27  WITH CHECK (auth.uid() = user_id);
28
29CREATE POLICY "Users can update own data"
30  ON your_table FOR UPDATE
31  USING (auth.uid() = user_id)
32  WITH CHECK (auth.uid() = user_id);
33
34CREATE POLICY "Users can delete own data"
35  ON your_table FOR DELETE
36  USING (auth.uid() = user_id);
37```
38
39## Migrations
40- Every change = new migration (never edit applied ones)
41- Test on dev Supabase first, always
42- Apply to prod only after dev confirmed

edge-functions.md

Supabase Edge Function patterns, templates, and deploy rules. Your backend engineer in a file.

markdown
 1---
 2description: Edge Function patterns and deployment rules.
 3alwaysApply: false
 4globs: ["supabase/functions/**/*.ts"]
 5---
 6
 7# Edge Function Rules
 8
 9## When to Use (vs API Routes)
10- AI API calls (Claude, OpenAI) → Edge Function
11- Webhook receivers (Stripe) → Edge Function
12- Scheduled jobs → Edge Function
13- Simple CRUD → API Route
14
15## Key Differences from Node.js
16- Runtime: Deno (not Node)
17- Imports: URL-based (esm.sh, deno.land)
18- Env: Deno.env.get('KEY')
19- No node_modules, no package.json
20
21## Standard Template
22```typescript
23import { serve } from 'https://deno.land/std@0.168.0/http/server.ts'
24import { createClient } from 'https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2'
25
26const corsHeaders = {
27  'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': '*',
28  'Access-Control-Allow-Headers':
29    'authorization, x-client-info, apikey, content-type',
30}
31
32serve(async (req) => {
33  if (req.method === 'OPTIONS')
34    return new Response('ok', { headers: corsHeaders })
35
36  try {
37    const supabase = createClient(
38      Deno.env.get('SUPABASE_URL') ?? '',
39      Deno.env.get('SUPABASE_ANON_KEY') ?? '',
40      { global: { headers: {
41        Authorization: req.headers.get('Authorization')! } } }
42    )
43    const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser()
44    if (!user) return new Response(
45      JSON.stringify({ error: 'Unauthorized' }),
46      { status: 401, headers: { ...corsHeaders,
47        'Content-Type': 'application/json' } })
48
49    const { input } = await req.json()
50    // Your logic here
51    return new Response(
52      JSON.stringify({ data: result }),
53      { headers: { ...corsHeaders,
54        'Content-Type': 'application/json' } })
55  } catch (error) {
56    return new Response(
57      JSON.stringify({ error: error.message }),
58      { status: 500, headers: { ...corsHeaders,
59        'Content-Type': 'application/json' } })
60  }
61})
62```
63
64## Secrets
65```bash
66supabase secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
67supabase secrets list
68```
69
70## Deploy
71```bash
72supabase functions deploy ai-chat
73supabase functions deploy stripe-webhook --no-verify-jwt
74```
75
76## Calling from Next.js
77```typescript
78const { data, error } = await supabase.functions.invoke(
79  'ai-chat', { body: { prompt, systemPrompt } })
80```
81
82## Scheduled (Cron)
83In supabase/config.toml:
84[functions.daily-digest]
85schedule = "0 9 * * *"  # 9am UTC daily

ux-patterns.md

UI/UX patterns for loading, error, empty states, and forms. Your design system in a file.

markdown
 1---
 2description: UI/UX patterns for states and forms.
 3alwaysApply: false
 4globs: ["components/**/*.tsx", "app/**/*.tsx"]
 5---
 6
 7# UX Patterns
 8
 9## The 3 States — Always Implement All 3
10```typescript
11if (loading) return <LoadingSpinner />
12if (error) return <ErrorMessage message={error} />
13if (!data || data.length === 0) return <EmptyState />
14// Then render content
15```
16
17## Forms
18- Disable submit while loading
19- Inline validation, not alerts
20- Clear form on success, preserve on error
21- Confirm destructive actions with modal
22
23## Mobile
24- Design for 375px first
25- Touch targets: min 44x44px
26- No hover-only interactions
27- Test on real device before shipping
Mark this section complete
Section 09

Configure Cursor to Remember Your Rules

PM Note

This is where everything clicks together. Once these rules are loaded, Cursor stops guessing and starts building exactly what your product needs. It's like giving a new engineer a 100-page onboarding doc — except Cursor actually reads it every time.

1

1Enable Project Rules

  • Open Cursor Settings: Cmd+, (Mac) or Ctrl+, (Windows)
  • Navigate: Features → Cursor Rules
  • Enable: "Project Rules (.cursor/rules)"

Global rules apply to all projects. Project rules apply only to this project. Always use project rules for product-specific guidance.

2

2Understand rule types

TypeWhen UsedBest For
AlwaysEvery conversationbuild, prd, guardrails, security
Auto AttachedWhen matching files are openapi-patterns, db-schema, edge-functions
Agent RequestedCursor decidesdiagnostic
ManualYou attach with @filenamedeep-dive sessions
3

3YAML frontmatter reference

Always:

yaml
---
description: [what this file is for]
alwaysApply: true
---

Auto-attached:

yaml
---
description: [what this file is for]
alwaysApply: false
globs: ["app/api/**/*.ts"]
---

Agent Requested:

yaml
---
description: [what this file is for]
alwaysApply: false
---
4

4Final configuration table

FileTypeGlob
build.mdAlways—
prd.mdAlways—
guardrails.mdAlways—
security.mdAlways—
diagnostic.mdAgent Requested—
api-patterns.mdAuto Attachedapp/api/**/*.ts
db-schema.mdAuto Attachedlib/supabase/**/*.ts
edge-functions.mdAuto Attachedsupabase/functions/**/*.ts
ux-patterns.mdAuto Attachedcomponents/**/*.tsx
5

5Verify Cursor reads your rules

Open a new Cursor chat and type:

prompt
What are the build rules for this project?
Summarize the tech stack and top 3 architecture rules.

Cursor should respond with content from your build.md. If not, check that alwaysApply: true is set.

6

6Power prompt template — use for every feature

prompt
@build @prd @security

Feature: [Feature name from PRD]

Context:
[What this feature does and who uses it]

Requirements:
- [Requirement 1]
- [Requirement 2]

Please:
1. Show me the file structure you'll create or modify
2. List any new database tables needed (with RLS policies)
3. List any Edge Functions needed
4. Then implement step by step, one file at a time

Follow all rules in the attached context files.
7

7Debug prompt template

prompt
@diagnostic @guardrails

Error: [paste the full error message]
File: [paste the file path]
Code: [paste the relevant code block]

Walk me through diagnosing this step by step.
Tell me what's wrong and show me the fix.
8

8Edge Function prompt template

prompt
@build @edge-functions @security

I need to create an Edge Function called [name].

What it does: [description]
Input: [what data it receives]
Output: [what data it returns]
Auth required: [yes/no]
Is it a webhook: [yes/no]

Follow all patterns in edge-functions.md.
Include: CORS headers, auth check, error handling, Deno.env secrets.
Mark this section complete
Section 10

Connect Supabase + Vercel + Cursor — One Pipeline

PM Note

This is the final wiring. After this, your entire workflow is: write in Cursor → push to GitHub → Vercel deploys automatically → correct database connects automatically. This used to require a DevOps team. Now it's an afternoon.

Part A: Connect Supabase to Vercel

  1. Go to Vercel Dashboard → your project → Settings → Integrations
  2. Search "Supabase" → Add Integration
  3. Authorize Vercel to access your Supabase org
  4. Connect dev Supabase project → Preview environment
  5. Connect prod Supabase project → Production environment
  6. Vercel auto-injects all Supabase env vars — verify in Settings → Environment Variables
  7. The magic: push to dev → preview URL uses dev database. Push to main → live app uses prod database. Fully automatic.

Part B: Link Vercel CLI

bash
# In Cursor terminal
vercel login
# Opens browser for OAuth

vercel link
# Select: link to existing project → choose yours

vercel env pull .env.local
# Pulls all Vercel env vars to your local .env.local

Part C: Link Supabase CLI

bash
# Login (opens browser)
supabase login

# Get project ref from dashboard URL:
# https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/[THIS-IS-YOUR-REF]
supabase link --project-ref your-dev-project-ref

# Pull current database schema
supabase db pull

# Push local schema changes
supabase db push

Part D: Set Edge Function Secrets

bash
# Dev project (already linked above)
supabase secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-your-key
supabase secrets set STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_test_your-key

# Switch to prod project
supabase link --project-ref your-prod-project-ref
supabase secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-your-key
supabase secrets set STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_your-key

# Switch back to dev for daily work
supabase link --project-ref your-dev-project-ref

Part E: The Complete Deploy Workflow

workflow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  DAILY BUILD WORKFLOW                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  1. Write code in Cursor (rules loaded, AI knows product)   │
│              ↓                                              │
│  2. Test locally: npm run dev → localhost:3000              │
│     (uses dev Supabase via .env.local)                      │
│              ↓                                              │
│  3. Commit + push to dev:                                   │
│     git add . → git commit → git push origin dev           │
│              ↓                                              │
│  4. Vercel auto-builds → Preview URL                       │
│     (uses dev Supabase automatically)                       │
│              ↓                                              │
│  5. Test preview → share with stakeholders                  │
│              ↓                                              │
│  6. Ready? Merge to main:                                   │
│     git checkout main → git merge dev → git push            │
│              ↓                                              │
│  7. Vercel auto-deploys to PRODUCTION                      │
│     (switches to prod Supabase automatically)               │
│              ↓                                              │
│  8. Live. Real users. Real database.                       │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Part F: Cursor MCP — Control Supabase Directly

PM Note

MCP is advanced but worth setting up. It lets you tell Cursor "create a users table" and it creates it directly in Supabase — no manual dashboard work.

Create .cursor/mcp.json:

json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "supabase": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": [
        "-y",
        "@supabase/mcp-server-supabase@latest",
        "--access-token",
        "YOUR_SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN"
      ]
    }
  }
}

Get your access token: supabase.com/dashboard/account/tokens → Generate new token → Name it "Cursor MCP" → Copy and paste into mcp.json.

Mark this section complete
Section 11

You're Set Up. Now Go Build.

PM Note

Setup is done. Most founders stop here. The ones who win open a new Cursor chat, paste one of the prompts below, and start building their first feature in the next 10 minutes. Be that founder.

Prompt 1 — Scaffold the entire app

prompt
@build @prd @security

Scaffold a production-ready Next.js 14 app with:
- App Router + TypeScript strict mode
- Tailwind CSS configured
- Supabase Auth (email + password)
- Login page, signup page, protected dashboard route
- Supabase client (browser + server) in /lib/supabase/
- Auth middleware in middleware.ts
- .env.example with all required variables

Follow all rules in build.md.
Start by showing the file structure, then implement one file at a time.

Prompt 2 — Build your first feature

prompt
@build @prd @api-patterns @db-schema @edge-functions

Feature: [Feature name]
User story: [paste from your prd.md]

Please:
1. Create the Supabase table with RLS policies
2. Create the Edge Function if AI is involved
3. Create the API route
4. Create the UI component with loading, error, empty states
5. Wire everything together

Follow all patterns in the attached rules.

Prompt 3 — Debug anything

prompt
@diagnostic @guardrails

Error I'm seeing:
[paste full error]

File where it's happening:
[paste file path]

Relevant code:
[paste code block]

Diagnose what's wrong, explain why it's happening,
and show me the exact fix.

Your Stack is Live When:

  • GitHub repo created with main + dev branches
  • Supabase dev and prod projects created with API keys saved
  • Vercel project connected to GitHub repo
  • All .md agent files created in .cursor/rules/
  • Cursor reading your rules (tested with a question)
  • Supabase + Vercel integration connected
  • vercel env pull .env.local run successfully
  • First feature built and deployed to preview URL

Strategy First. Prompt Second. Results Always.

OrbitumAI — orbitumai.com

Mark this section complete
